coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as a novel conductive additive to improve the capacitive performance for supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Yongfang
Wang, Jing
Zhang, Jin
Shi, Zhiqiang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a novel conductive agent with nanometer-scale instead of the common carbon black (CB) offer a promising round to construct an effective conductive network for the improvement of the supercapacitors' capacitive performance. Interestingly, the electrochemical performance of activated carbon (AC)-based composite electrodes with different amounts of GQDs in two composite ways was tested, which showed the extremely apparent correlation between the electrode structure and the capacitive characteristics. It was found that the specific capacitance (Cm) and rate performance were enhanced when GQDs at an appropriate weight ratio (1 wt%) in the electrode material, compared with 10 wt% CB, and the corresponding Cm values are 110 and 100 F g−1 at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 and 85 and 65 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, respectively. Furthermore, composite electrodes by heating treatment displayed the better electronic conductivity and cycling stability, which was caused by removing oxygen functional groups, at the expense of decline for the ionic conductivity and rate performance. This work demonstrates an effective method to replace the conventional conductive agent to improve the electrochemical performance of the supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Conductive additive
Supercapacitors
Oxygen functional groups

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of AC (a), HRTEM image of GQDs (b,c), (d–f) SEM images of AC-HG5 powders (d), and electrode's side section (e) AC-CB, (f) AC-HG5.
XPS pattern of GQDs (a) and rGQDs (c); C1s XPS spectra of GQDs (b), rGQDs (d) and AC-G1, AC-HG1 and AC (e); XRD pattern of GQDs (f).

Galvanostatic charge/discharge cycling tests of the AC-G, AC-HG, AC-CB sample electrodes, (a,b) charge/discharge curves at 0.1 A g−1; (c,d) Rate performances; (e,f) Ragone plots; The voltage window are 2.7–0.0 V, 1.5 M L−1 TEMA-BF4/PC electrolytes.
Rate performances of the AC-G1, AC-HG1, AC-CB electrodes.
(a–d) Cyclic voltammograms of the AC-G, AC-HG, AC-CB electrodes in scan rates of 1 mV s−1 and 200 mV s−1.

(a–c) Nyquist plots of the AC-G, AC-HG, AC-CB type electrodes obtained from different test conditions, (a) in open circuit potential, (b) after GCD test in current density of 1 A g−1, and (c) fitting data of AC-G1 electrode, the illustration is fitting circuit diagram.

Cycling performance of the AC-G and AC-HG type electrodes using current density of 0.5 A g−1 at 25 °C, the illustration is the 10000th galvanostatic charge and discharge curves of AC-G1 and AC-HG1 electrode at the corresponding temperature.

Cycling performance of the AC-G and AC-HG type electrodes using current density of 0.5 A g−1 at 65 °C, the illustration is the 3000th galvanostatic charge and discharge curves of AC-G1 and AC-HG1 electrode at the corresponding temperature.
The 3000th galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of AC-G1(a) and AC-HG1(b) electrode at 25 °C and 65 °C at current density of 0.5 A g−1.
Fitting data of the sample electrodes.
Element content values of various electrodes.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as a novel conductive additive to improve the capacitive performance for supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Advanced Fibers and Energy Storage, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Advanced Fibers and Energy Storage
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a novel conductive agent with nanometer-scale instead of the common carbon black (CB) offer a promising round to construct an effective conductive network for the improvement of the supercapacitors' capacitive performance. Interestingly, the electrochemical performance of activated carbon (AC)-based composite electrodes with different amounts of GQDs in two composite ways was tested, which showed the extremely apparent correlation between the electrode structure and the capacitive characteristics. It was found that the specific capacitance (Cm) and rate performance were enhanced when GQDs at an appropriate weight ratio (1 wt%) in the electrode material, compared with 10 wt% CB, and the corresponding Cm values are 110 and 100 F g−1 at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 and 85 and 65 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, respectively. Furthermore, composite electrodes by heating treatment displayed the better electronic conductivity and cycling stability, which was caused by removing oxygen functional groups, at the expense of decline for the ionic conductivity and rate performance. This work demonstrates an effective method to replace the conventional conductive agent to improve the electrochemical performance of the supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors as a new type of energy storage devices have been attracting much attention because of the excellent cycle life and high power densities [1–3]. They have wide applications from portable power tools to hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) that require fast charging and discharging [4,5]. Electrode as the substrate material for charge and electrolyte ion storage is crucial for the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors. Carbon-based materials are the most common electrode materials for electric double layer capacitors (EDLC), including activated carbon (AC), activated carbon fiber, carbon nanotubes, carbon aerogels, carbon template graphene [6,7]. Among them, AC has been used as the electrodes for commercial supercapacitors due to its large specific surface area, rich pore structure, low price and stable structure [2,4]. However, the specific capacitance for AC electrodes would be significantly reduced if no conductive agent is added, because of the low electronic conductivity of AC itself [8,9]. Conductive agent includes granular carbon black, metal powder and graphene quantum dots; fibrous nanotubes and carbon fiber; flake graphite and graphene, carbon onions, or two conductive agent synergy [10–14]. Generally, the literatures reported an appropriate amount for most conductive additive at about 5 mass% [12,13]. Conductive agent could enhance the electronic conductivity, even to construct appropriate 3D pore structure for charge storage and diffusion, increase the ionic conductivity, and then improve the rate and cycling performance for supercapacitors.

Graphene has received increasing interest for EDLC application on account of its excellent properties including large theoretical surface area (2630 m2 g−1), outstanding chemical and electrochemical stability, high electrical conductivity [15–18], which makes it possible as a conductive additive for electrodes. In 2012, Jiang [19] used graphene nanosheets as a conductive additive to improve the capacitive performance of α-MnO2, and mainly took advantage of the synergy effect between the “plane-to-point” mode (due to graphene nanosheets) and “filling” mode (due to acetylene black). The intrinsic advantages of graphene can enhance the high-rate capabilities as a conductive additive [20]. Graphene with specific two-dimensional layered structure has an evident effect on its conductivity, so as for the composite way of graphene and active materials [8,21,22]. Surface functional groups on the graphene would react with the electrolyte and the decomposition products may cause pore blocking, which greatly affects the performance stability of supercapacitors [23]. Compared to heat reduction of graphene oxide after the liquid phase mixing, the way with reduction followed by mixing would have more micropores to increase specific capacitance of the material. Moreover, graphene dispersed in liquid could be well dispersed between the activated carbon particles, so as to construct three-dimensional structure, increase the effective electrochemical surface area, improve the electrolyte wettability at high discharge current density, and acquire better electrochemical performance finally.

However, the conductive effect for the conductive agent is limited by some factors. In general, conductive agent itself does not contribute to the capacity, and its density is also low. Hence, excessive conductive agent content will cause the specific capacitance in weight and volume of the active materials decreased. In addition, superfluous content of conductive agent will agglomerate and even block pores in activated carbon, thus affecting the diffusion and infiltration for electrolyte ions [19,24,25]. Therefore, the content of conductive agent should be controlled. Furthermore, it is very difficult for ions to penetrate through the hexagonal carbon ring in the layered structure of graphene (called “steric effect”), hindering the performance of supercapacitors under high-rate conditions [26,27]. In this investigation, we adopted extremely small quantity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as conductive agent, using common carbon black as reference. GQDs are typically defined for the size of <100 nm, the thickness of graphene in 10 layers or less, and exhibit stronger quantum confinement effect and boundary effect, which are applied widely in photovoltaic devices, bio-medicine, emitting diodes and sensors [28,29]. Controllable preparation of GQDs includes top-down and bottom-up two methods [30,31]. GQDs with advantages of small size assemble, high surface area, high conductivity, rich functional groups, as well as excellent solvent dispersion and edge effect, can be used for constructing superior Micro-Supercapacitors with outstanding electrochemical performance [32,33].

In our work, GQDs was used as conductive agent in the activated carbon-based supercapacitors at different weight ratio (≤5 wt%). Simultaneously, the relationship between different composite ways of GQDs and AC and electrochemical performance of the composite electrodes were studied. It's was interesting that the electrochemical performance was effectively enhanced when GQDs existed in the electrodes at an appropriate weight ratio and thermal reduction for composite electrodes is helpful for the cycling stability, especially in high temperature environments. Different composite methods would cause variable surface chemistry and microstructures on the electrodes. The functional groups with GQDs decline the in-plane conductivity, but may accelerate the electron transfer and ion transport owing to favorable wettability with electrolyte. However, the oxygen functional groups would impact the cycle stability inevitably [23,34]. What's more, a larger amount of the conductive agents would improve electronic conductivity at the expense of the ionic conductivity.

The activated carbon (YP-50F) used as active material of the electrode, was commercial available (Kuraray Chemical Co. Ltd., Japan); graphene quantum dots (2 mg ml−1) was provided by Qingdao Haida Haixi New Materials Co., Ltd., China. Electrolyte (1.5 M L−1 TEMABF4/PC) was purchased from Shenzhen CAPCHEM Technology Co. Ltd.

Liquid phase mixing electrodes (marked as AC-G) were prepared by mixing a certain amount of AC, GQDs solution adopted as conductive agent at various amount (0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt% and 5 wt% of AC weight, respectively), and the binder of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) at 8 wt% of total weight. The homogeneous slurry was regulated in rubbery, followed by pressed into a thin film with the thickness of about 100 μm through roller presses, and then compressed into aluminium (Al) foil collector. The coin cells (R2430) were symmetrically assembled with two equivalent electrodes punched into 13 mm in diameter. The average mass of electrodes subtract the mass of aluminium (Al) foil collector were about 7–8 mg. The type of the separator is NKK TF45, and the amount of electrolyte is about 0.6–0.8 ml. As a contrast, electrodes prepared by thermal reduction process after liquid mixing (AC and GQDs at same proportion as the above) were named as AC-HG. 2 g AC and a certain amount of GQDs in the same ratio as above were added to a crucible (30 ml). The mixture was dried in the oven before putted into tube furnace. The reduction process was carried out in H2/Ar atmosphere at a flow rate of 200 ml/min. The mixture was heated to 250 °C at a ramp rate of 1 °C/min, then heated to 600 °C at 5 °C/min, followed by maintaining 600 °C for 3 h, and then adding PTFE of 8 wt% of total weight to prepare electrode with same procedure. For convenience, these electrodes were signed as AC-G0.5, AC-G1, AC-G2 and AC-G5; AC-HG0.5, AC-HG1, AC-HG2 and AC-HG5. The reference electrodes adopting 10 wt% common carbon black (G-VXC-72) as conductive agent, were named as AC-CB.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Tecnai G2 F20) were used to observe the morphology and structure of GQDs and composite electrodes. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a Rigaku D/Max2500 X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kαradiation. Qualitative and quantitative analysis for surface chemical composition was investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Galvanostatic charge/discharge (Arbin, USA) tests were carried out at current densities ranging from 0.1 A g−1 to 10.0 A g−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Autolab PGSTAT128N, Switzerland) plots were acquired in the frequency range from 100 kHz to 1 mHz at an ac amplitude of 0.005 V. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) (CHI604D, China) curves were tested at diverse scan rates (1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200 mV s−1). The cycle life measurements were carried out at current density of 0.5 A g−1 at 2.7 V in thermostat. The Cm values were calculated by the following formula:
(1)
C m
=
2 I Δ t
m Δ V
in which I was the current, Δt was the discharge time, ΔV was the voltage window get rid of the ohmic drop, and m is the mass of active material in the single electrode.

The energy density was calculated from the equation:
(2)
E =
1 2
CV 2
in which C was the specific capacitance of the capacitors, V was the operating voltage of the capacitor.

The power density was calculated using the equation:
(3)
P =
I Δ V
2 m
where I was the discharge current, ΔV was the voltage window from the end of the ohmic drop to the end of the discharge process, and m is the active material mass of the single electrode.

Morphologies and microstructures of GQDs and AC powders were characterized with SEM and TEM. AC powders are irregular micron-size particles with rich pore distributed on the surface as seen in Fig. 1a. From the HRTEM image in Fig. 1b and c, we observed quantum dots structure with uniform size of about 5 nm, and striped structure arranged in orderly with layer distance of 0.34 nm. Due to the small size and very little amounts of graphene, the morphology distinction for the composites with different GQDs additive agents is not obvious. Taking 5 wt% GQDs additive agents as an example, the morphology of AC particles mixed with GQDs solution followed by thermal reduction (AC-HG5) maintains block structure as shown in Fig. 1d, smaller size particles were attached to the surface of AC and filled between the particles, which will be beneficial for electron conductivity. Micro-structure and morphologies of the electrodes were characterized with SEM (Fig. 1e–f) to investigate the influence of the electrode structure on the electrochemical performance. As shown in Fig. 1e, 10 wt% CB as conductive agent, it was seen that a mass of small particles filled in the gap and covered on the surface of AC particles to improve electron conduction. Nevertheless, excessive amount of conductive agents would be agglomerated and block the cavity structure of AC, reducing the effective adsorption area of AC. When adding 5 wt% GQDs (AC-HG5), corresponding to Fig. 1f, there were less small particles and a small quantity of GQDs attached to the surface of the AC, which is conducive to ion adsorption and desorption during charging and discharging process. At the same time, a small amount of conductive agents would increase the proportion of active material in the electrode and effectively improve the weight and volume energy density of the device. In addition, for the samples with heat treatment (AC-HG), conductive agent and activated carbon particles could blend more homogeneous in the electrodes.

In order to characterize the chemical structure, GQDs was measured by XPS measurements. Fig. 2a shows that there are abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in GQDs. Through fitting the C1s XPS spectra of GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2b, we found that the carboxyl (COOH) and hydroxy (COH) accounts for a larger proportion than the carbon-carbon double bonds (CC), the specific data are 62.47%, 14.32% and 23.20% in sequence. It's related to the preparation methods of GQDs, and the boundary effect from much bare edge, which could connect a large number of functional groups, therefore the value of C/O just reaches to 1.02 in Table 1 . The oxygen-containing functional groups from GQDs are reduced significantly after heat treatment (named as rGQDs), as shown in Fig. 2c and d. In addition, it remains some oxygen-containing functional groups in the composites of GQDs and AC, taking 1% for example, as shown in Fig. 2e. Among them, the C/O value of AC-HG1 is a little higher than that of AC-G1 sample. From XRD patterns of GQDs in Fig. 2f, a evident diffraction peak (002) at 2 θ ≈ 26° is observed, corresponding to the representative peak for carbon material. The peak can be seen as a combination of broad peaks (15° to 35°) and a sharp peak (27.3°), indicating that GQDs presents a graphene-like amorphous structure and retains part of the graphite structure.

Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) cycling tests of different types of composite electrodes were performed to explore the conductive effect of GQDs on the electrochemical property of AC-based supercapacitors. Fig. 3a and b show the GCD curves of AC-G, AC-HG, AC-CB electrodes at current density of 0.1 A g−1. GCD profiles at different current density are showed in Fig. S3. All the samples show a linear and nearly symmetric profile, suggesting excellent electrochemical reversibility and the dominant contribution is from EDLC. In addition, almost no IR drop is observed when capacitors begin to discharge, which indicates the resistance of electrodes is very small. Furthermore, it's clear that the charging and discharging time for the AC-G and AC-HG series electrodes is longer than that of AC-CB electrode, which shows that tiny amounts of GQDs (≤5 wt%) as conductive agent, compared to the commercial CB (10 wt%), is more conducive to the capacity improvement, and the maximum Cm values are 110 and 100 F g−1, respectively. The Cm values at different current densities range from 0.1 to 10 A g−1 are given in Fig. 3c and d. The corresponding Cm values are varying with the amounts of GQDs used in the electrode materials at high current density. Among them, rate performance for both AC-G and AC-HG electrodes, the 1 wt% addition is the best, and better than that of AC-CB electrodes. The analysis results from Ragon plots in Fig. 3e and f are consistent with the above capacitance results. When power density reaches to 11.6 kW kg−1, the energy density for electrodes with 1 wt% addition of GQDs and AC-CB electrodes are 14.7 Wh kg−1 and 11.3 Wh kg−1 respectively.

The larger van der Waals forces between graphene layers make it easier to agglomerate. When GQDs amounts increase, it is not beneficial to construct an efficient conductive network. The favorable “filling effect” becomes unpromising “blocking effect” and thereby the electron transfer and ion transport become difficult and the rate capability decline. Furthermore, excessive additives would reduce the proportion of active materials in electrodes, decreasing the specific capacitance in weight and volume ultimately. In addition, we found thermal reduction process has an effect on electrochemical performance. The capacitive performance for AC-G-type electrodes is superior to that of AC-HG-type electrodes, especially when the addition amounts increase, taking AC-G1 and AC-HG1 electrodes as examples. In Fig. 4 , the Cm values for AC-G1, AC-HG1 and AC-CB type electrodes are 110 F g−1, 105 F g−1 and 100 F g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 current density, respectively. When increasing the current density to 10 A g−1, their Cm values drop by 22.7%, 28.6% and 35%, respectively. It's found that the capacitive performance for AC-G1 is most excellent, followed by AC-HG1, and better than AC-CB electrode. During thermal reduction process, rich oxygen-containing functional groups were eliminated, which weaken the wettability of electrodes to electrolyte, microcrystalline structure and defects changes, simultaneously, all of which make the capacity and rate performance decline.

Fig. 5 shows cyclic voltammograms of AC-G, AC-HG and AC-CB electrodes at sweep speed of 1 mV s−1 and 200 mV s−1. CV plots of electrodes with different amounts of GQDs all present good rectangularity. Comparing the CV curves of AC-G1, AC-HG1 and AC-CB in Fig. 5c, the curve area for AC-CB electrode is smallest, indicating the lowest capacitance value. When the scan rates reached to 200 mV s−1, the polarization phenomenon for AC-HG1 electrode is the most serious, describing poor rate performance. As for AC-CB electrode, the polarization of the curve is most gradual and the area is the smallest, indicating the lowest resistance and smallest capacitance value, which is owing to the more conductive agent added. The cyclic voltammograms and specific capacitance of AC-G1, AC-HG1 and AC-CB electrodes at different scan rates (1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200 mV s−1) are showed in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2. It’s indicated that the capacitance values are higher for electrodes with 1 wt% additive agent of GQDs, compared with the AC-CB electrode, which is in accordance with galvanostatic charge-discharge values.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an important tool to research the electrode surface phenomenon and internal reaction during electric double layer capacitors working process. The Nyquist plots in Fig. 6 reflect typical EDLC characteristics. The intercept on the X-axis in the high frequency range, is the series resistance (RS) that generally derived from separator, electrolyte, and external circuit resistance [35,36]. The semi-circle is the interfacial contact resistance (RCT) between electrode and electrolyte, and the straight line with slope of 45° in medium frequency region is the Warburg conductance (YW) originating from the diffusion of electrolyte ions. The vertical line in the low frequency range represents the capacitance characteristics. Using 1 wt% additive agent of GQDs as an example, 5 wt% as a comparison. As shown in Fig. 6a, the resistance values for all electrodes are small, explaining excellent conductivity. It's also found that the more GQDs added, the smaller resistance value, which stated that GQDs could improve the materials' conductivity effectively. In addition, the RCT for AC-HG type electrodes is the smallest, <5 Ω, compared to AC-G and AC-CB electrodes. It means the structure of composite material with thermal reduction treatment is more suitable for the transport of charge carriers and electron transmission, which accounts for the elimination of oxygen containing functional groups from GQDs, more complete structure and fewer defects after heat treatment process. It's consistent with the previous results of the GCD tests. Surface functional groups could improve the infiltration of the material to the electrolyte, and reduce the ion transport resistance, which will improve its rate performance, but also increase its charge transfer resistance. The specific values obtained through fitting the impedance circuit, as shown in Fig. 6c, are displayed in Table 2 . After repeated charging and discharging test at current density of 1 A g−1, the RCT decreases for AC-G1 electrode, which could well be ascribed to the elimination of functional groups resulting from reaction with the electrolyte [23].

The long cycle life is vital feature for supercapacitors, which was displayed in Fig. 7 . The supercapacitors based on different type electrodes all exhibit excellent cycle stability performances and retain 85% of its initial capacity through 10,000 cycles. The discharge Cm values of AC-G electrodes were found to be slightly lower than that of AC-HG electrodes, 65 F g−1 and 73 F g−1 respectively, which can also be confirmed by the charge and discharge curves in the illustration. The structure for AC-HG samples with reduction treatment is more stable and equipped with less oxygen-containing functional groups, which is beneficial to improve its cycle stability [23,34].

Supercapacitors can operate in a wide operating temperature range from −40–70 °C, so cycling performance at high environmental temperatures is also worthy of attention. GCD tests of AC-G and AC-HG electrode at 65 °C were performed in the high-low temperature test chamber with current density of 0.5 A g−1, the results are shown in Fig. 8 . Discharge capacity decreases drastically at first 200 cycles, especially for that of AC-G electrodes, and remains stable thereafter. The recession in capacitance could well be due to the consumption of electrolyte resulting from the irreversible reaction between the electrolyte and electrodes. The Cm values of AC-G1 and AC-G5 electrodes remain only 46.5 and 34.4 F g−1 and retention rates are 62.2% and 51.6% after 3600 cycles, respectively. In contrast, the Cm values of AC-HG1 and AC-HG5 electrodes are 71.7 and 61.6 F g−1 and retention rates are 81.6% and 69.3%, respectively. The charge and discharge curve in the illustration of Fig. 8 can also states that the discharge time of AC-HG1 electrode is about double of AC-G1 electrode's time after 3000 cycles. Simultaneously, it's found that increasing the GQDs amounts in electrodes is adverse to the cycling stability, as a result of the more oxygen contents in the electrodes.

The change in temperature has a double effect on the capacity and internal resistance of the supercapacitors. The ion adsorption process in the double electric layer principle of supercapacitors is sensitive to heat. When the temperature rises, the ion activity increases, which is not conducive to the occurrence of stable adsorption reaction. The amount of charge adsorbed on the same area is reduced and the capacity is reduced. On the other hand, when the temperature rises, the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte increases and the internal resistance decreases, so the capacity increases [37]. GCD curves of AC-G1 and AC-HG1 electrodes at 25 °C and 65 °C environment after repeated 3000 times are given in Fig. 9a and b, respectively. The perfect linear and symmetrical curves imply ideal capacitive behavior of the electrodes in both room temperature and high temperature environment. It is noteworthy that discharge time for AC-G1 electrode in 65 °C is significantly less than that in 25 °C, and the corresponding Cm values are reduced. In contrast, discharge time for AC-HG1 electrode in 65 °C is not much shorter than that in 25 °C, so as the specific capacity values shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8. These results indicate the influence of temperature on the adsorption process dominates the main factors that affect the capacity of the supercapacitors. In addition, the high temperature environment would aggravate the irreversible consumption between electrolyte ions and electrodes. Therefore the Cm values of the electrodes decrease in high temperature. Furthermore, the oxygen-containing functional groups on AC-G electrodes become more active at high temperatures, prone to irreversible redox reactions with the electrolyte. This process produces gas, reduces adsorbable area, and hinders the charging course at the electrode interface [38], resulting in the decline of Cm for AC-G electrodes at first 200 cycles and negative effect on cycling stability. This argument can also be verified by the EDX data in Table 3 . The sample named as AC-G1-Cycle and AC-HG1-Cycle are the corresponding electrodes after cycling test at 65 °C. The oxygen contents for AC-G1-Cycle and AC-HG1-Cycle electrodes are reduced, especially for the former. It is concluded that the composite electrodes with heat treatment is more stable and conducive to improve cycle performance, especially in high temperature environment.

GQDs were used as a novel and effective conductive agent in the AC based-supercapacitors. The specific capacitance and rate performance were enhanced when GQDs at an appropriate weight ratio (1 wt%) in the electrode material, compared with 10 wt% CB. In addition, the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups from GQDs could improve ion conductivity and rate performance for the composite electrodes, but is adverse to electron conductivity and cycle stability, especially at high temperature environment. It is noteworthy that the technology to prepare graphene is important for its application. This work also provides a new direction for the application of GQDs in the field of activated carbon-based supercapacitors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (51603147); Tianjin Application Foundation and Advanced Technology Research Plan Project (14RCHZGX00859, 14JCTPJC00484, 14JCQNJC07200); and Tianjin Science and Technology Support Program Key Project (15ZCZDGX00270).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.09.003.
